b' Money Stuff
LieborThe way that global financial markets worked for decades is that trillions of dollars of floating-rate loans and interest-rate derivat

Liebor

The way that global financial markets worked for decades is that trillions of dollars of floating-rate loans and interest-rate derivatives were indexed to Libor, the London interbank offered rate. And the way Libor worked was that someone \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0at the time relevant to our story\xc2\xa0it was the British Bankers\xe2\x80\x99 Association[1] \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0would call up a group of big international banks and ask them \xe2\x80\x9chow much would you have to pay right now to borrow dollars for one month,\xe2\x80\x9d and they\xe2\x80\x99d answer, and the BBA would take some trimmed average of their answers, and that was one-month dollar Libor. And there were other Libors for other currencies and tenors, computed in the same way.

Most important financial benchmarks do not work this way. The S&P 500 index is not calculated by calling some banks and saying \xe2\x80\x9chey how\xc2\xa0much would you pay for these 500 stocks\xe2\x80\x9d and averaging their answers. The S&P 500 index is calculated based on the last trade of each stock on a public stock exchange. Most indexes are based on actual trades.[2] But Libor was invented because it was very useful , for the floating-rate loan business, to have an index of banks\xe2\x80\x99 unsecured borrowing costs , and unsecured short-term bank debt did not really trade on a transparent public exchange. It traded\xc2\xa0in an informal, telephone-based interbank market, and the easiest way to find out what trades banks were doing was to call them.

Also, though, they didn\xe2\x80\x99t do trades in every Libor tenor and currency every day. If you wanted to know how much a bank would pay to borrow Danish kroner for two months \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0which was a real Libor rate \xe2\x80\x94 you could not compute that every day based on how much each Libor bank actually paid to borrow kroner for two months that day. Big international banks did not borrow kroner for two months every day.\xc2\xa0Certainly not every day at exactly the time the BBA called them to ask. But a bank which had borrowed kroner for one month an hour ago, and borrowed dollars for three months five minutes ago, and had a general sense of the curve of its various short-term borrowing costs, could probably give you a good guess at how much it would have to pay to borrow kroner for two months right now. So you could just call it and ask it for that guess. Libor was \xe2\x80\x9cthe rate at which banks don't lend to each other,\xe2\x80\x9d people said .

The problem is that Libor also became the rate underpinning trillions of dollars of derivatives contracts, and the banks traded those derivatives and built up large positions. And some days it would be good for the derivatives traders to have a low Libor \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0they had to pay Libor on a bunch of contracts resetting that day, so a low Libor would let them pay less \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and other days it would be good for the derivatives traders to have a high Libor. And the derivatives traders realized that, if it was worth $1,000,000 to their bank to have Libor be one basis point lower, then it was worth $200,000 to their bonus to have Libor be one basis point lower, which meant that it was worth calling up their bank\xe2\x80\x99s Libor submitter \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the person who answered the phone when the BBA called \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and saying \xe2\x80\x9chey mate I\xe2\x80\x99ll buy you a case of Champagne if you submit a lower Libor.\xe2\x80\x9d And then the BBA would call the submitter and say \xe2\x80\x9cwhere can you borrow yen for three months,\xe2\x80\x9d and the submitter would say \xe2\x80\x9coh 0.525\xe2\x80\x9d when in his heart of hearts he knew\xc2\xa0that the real answer was 0.545, and the BBA would average that in, and 3-month yen Libor would be a smidge\xc2\xa0lower than it would otherwise have been, and the bank would make money on the derivatives, and the derivatives traders would get their bonuses and the submitter would get his Champagne .

This went on for a while and eventually regulators and prosecutors noticed it and it became a huge huge huge scandal[3] and:

  1. Banks were fined billions and billions of dollars for rigging Libor;
  2. Bank regulators decided\xc2\xa0to get rid of Libor and replace it with other rates that are harder to rig; and
  3. Various derivatives traders were arrested and prosecuted for rigging Libor.

Point 1 is sort of self-explanatory. In the post-crisis period, if banks do shady stuff and get caught, they pay big fines. Point 2 is quite complicated, because the issues we discussed above \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that banks do not actually borrow unsecured in every tenor and currency every day, that the bank funding market is not very transparent, etc. \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0remain true. The way regulators in the U.S. have addressed these issues is to replace Libor with a benchmark interest\xc2\xa0rate called SOFR , the Secured Overnight Financing Rate,\xc2\xa0which reflects the cost of borrowing money for one day secured by Treasury securities. That rate can be calculated based on actual transactions in a liquid market. I should say, regulators are in the slow process of replacing Libor with SOFR; I am talking about Libor in the past tense but that is a bit misleading and it is still in wide use despite being phased out. This is partly for reasons of inertia, but also because using an overnight secured rate\xc2\xa0raises its own issues for floating-rate lending, etc., which we have talked about before but will not talk about again here.

Here we will talk about Point 3. Some bank traders were prosecuted for rigging Libor.[4] In the U.S., they were prosecuted for \xe2\x80\x9cwire fraud,\xe2\x80\x9d the crime of lying to people to make money.[5]

There is something a little bit odd about this, if you think about it too hard. The BBA would call up a bank\xe2\x80\x99s Libor submitter and say \xe2\x80\x9cwhere could you borrow yen\xc2\xa0for three months\xe2\x80\x9d and the submitter would say \xe2\x80\x9c0.525\xe2\x80\x9d when the correct answer was 0.545. But what does it mean to say that the correct answer was 0.545? It does not mean that, as the submitter was saying \xe2\x80\x9c0.525,\xe2\x80\x9d he was simultaneously entering into a three-month unsecured borrowing agreement to borrow dollars at 0.545%. It means that the submitter\xe2\x80\x99s unbiased best guess about what the bank would pay, right that minute, to borrow a reasonable amount of dollars for three months, was 0.545%, but he said 0.525% due to impure motives.

But of course the submitter\xe2\x80\x99s unbiased best guess was just a guess about a hypothetical question, and for most Libor tenors and currencies on most days that guess would not line up precisely with a contemporaneous actual transaction. And so you could not prove that that best guess \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa00.545% here \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0was \xe2\x80\x9ccorrect,\xe2\x80\x9d that it reflected the true answer to the BBA\xe2\x80\x99s question, which was to submit \xe2\x80\x9cthe rate at which [the bank]\xc2\xa0could borrow funds, were it to do so by asking for\xc2\xa0and then accepting inter-bank offers in reasonable market size just prior to [11 a.m. London time].\xe2\x80\x9d[6]

Which means that you also could not prove that the biased, dishonest, Champagne-induced guess \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa00.525% here \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0was false . Could the bank have borrowed yen for three months at 0.525% around the time that the BBA called? I don\xe2\x80\x99t know! Perhaps you could go back and find proof that, right around that time, the bank was looking to borrow yen for three months, and it got a range of offers, and they were all in a range of 0.543% to 0.548% and the 0.525% was a lie. But there is no obvious reason to think that would always be the case; for the most part these were judgment calls and you won\xe2\x80\x99t find a smoking gun proving that the 0.525% was a lie.

More to the point, no one, uh, looked? These cases were brought based mainly on really bad chats . What happened is that prosecutors would investigate a bank\xe2\x80\x99s Libor dealings and find electronic chats like this:

Derivatives trader: Hey what Libor are you submitting today?

Submitter: I think the right number is 0.525%.

Derivatives trader: It would help me out a lot, in terms of profiting on my derivatives, if you would lie and submit a higher number.

Submitter: What if I lied and submitted 0.545%, which is not what I think is the right number?

Derivatives trader: Perfect, I appreciate it, in exchange for your dishonest criminal conduct I will happily pay you a bribe of one case of Champagne.

Submitter: Your bribe is acceptable to me, pleasure doing crime with you.

Derivatives trader: Hope we don\xe2\x80\x99t go to jail!

Submitter: Lol. We should, though, what with the crimes we\xe2\x80\x99re doing!

And then they would charge the trader with wire fraud, and the jury would look at the chats and be like \xe2\x80\x9cwell yes this sounds like wire fraud\xe2\x80\x9d and convict them. Seriously the chats are bad !

Two former Deutsche Bank AG traders named\xc2\xa0Matthew Connolly and Gavin Black were convicted in a New York federal court of wire fraud for manipulating Libor, and they appealed their convictions,\xc2\xa0pointing out that there was no proof that the numbers they submitted were wrong . And last week the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit agreed with them and tossed out their convictions . Maybe the numbers were right! Who can say? The numbers were necessarily subjective, and anyway the prosecutors forgot to prove they were wrong.

Here is the Second Circuit\xe2\x80\x99s opinion . Connolly and Black traded interest-rate derivatives and cash products, and were accused of\xc2\xa0pressuring Deutsche Bank\xe2\x80\x99s Libor submitters to submit wrong Libors. Two Deutsche Bank Libor submitters cooperated with the government and testified against them. These were James King and Michael Curtler, who worked on a cash money market trading desk, \xe2\x80\x9cborrowing money in the interbank\xc2\xa0market in order to fund DB's cash needs.\xe2\x80\x9d

The way the cash desk worked is that it would borrow money in the interbank market and then lend it to other Deutsche Bank units to fund their operations;\xc2\xa0the cash desk would show a real, live, market-based price to those other units.\xc2\xa0King and Curtler had little spreadsheets (called \xe2\x80\x9cpricers\xe2\x80\x9d)\xc2\xa0that they used to decide what that price would be, and they would use that price \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the price that they charged Deutsche Bank\xe2\x80\x99s other units \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0as the basis for their Libor submission. Though sometimes they\xe2\x80\x99d bump it up or down a bit based on their market judgment. From the opinion (citations omitted):

In addition to the above alterations in the pricer, King each day\xc2\xa0consulted, inter alia, five interbank cash brokers before settling on DB's LIBOR\xc2\xa0submissions.\xc2\xa0He testified that it was logical to "change the [LIBOR\xc2\xa0submission] rates . . . so that they lined up with what the brokers were predicting" because "the brokers have access to all the banks, they know where we can borrow money or they think they know where we can borrow money." But King said that while sometimes the rates the brokers were suggesting were all similar, "there [we]re periods when the rates are vastly different"; and then "[we] have to come up with something that we feel is a fair reflection of our rate." Thus, in submitting LIBOR rates, "some days [King] went with the pricer, some days [he] went with the broker, [and] some days [he] went with the middle." King testified that he believed that the reference to "reasonable market size" in the BBA LIBOR Instruction--a term the BBA did not define--"gave [him] flexibility as to where [he] could actually submit his LIBOR."

So he made up a number based on his feel for the market, his own borrowing activity in the interbank market, his own lending activity in the intra -bank market, and calling some interbank brokers and getting their feel for the market.

And then he\xe2\x80\x99d sometimes change that number to benefit the derivatives traders:

King\xc2\xa0testified that "Gavin Black occasionally asked me to manipulate the rates or to put in a submission that was higher or lower than I would have done to benefit his trading position," and that "Matthew Connolly on occasion made requests for me to change our LIBOR rates and to benefit the trader's position." \xe2\x80\xa6

The government also introduced other emails and electronic messages to DB's LIBOR submitters requesting modifications of the LIBOR submissions in order to benefit DB's existing trading positions. They included one from Black on May 15, 2008, requesting a low 1-month LIBOR rate because he was "paying on 18 [billion]"; another from Black on February 21, 2005, requesting a high 6-month LIBOR rate; one from Connolly on November 23, 2005, requesting that 3-month LIBOR "be as high as possible"; and an August 12, 2007 email from Connolly stating, "[i]f possible, we need in NY 1mo libor as low as possible next few days....tons of pays coming up overall.\xe2\x80\x9d As a result of this last request, King's DB submission to LIBOR on August 13 was the lowest by any panel bank; and the DB submission on August 15 was four basis points below his estimate of what LIBOR would be.

As Libor-manipulation emails go these are not particularly bad \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0they don\xe2\x80\x99t mention Champagne, or prison\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but they clearly fit the prosecutors\xe2\x80\x99 narrative of \xe2\x80\x9cbanks submitted wrong Libors to make money on derivatives.\xe2\x80\x9d Also it sounds bad. Also the Libor submitters said \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0on the witness stand, at trial, after signing non-prosecution agreements \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that it was bad:

All three cooperators testified that they "knew" the practice of altering DB's LIBOR submissions to benefit DB trader positions was "wrong" at the time they engaged in it. King stated that "[i]t's intuitively wrong because we are, you know, as I say, taking advantage of the position. We are benefiting. There was a counterparty on the other side who doesn't know what we're doing and is being affected negatively by what we're doing."\xc2\xa0Parietti testified that "even if [LIBOR is] imprecise, hard to\xc2\xa0estimate, or vague, . . . it's still wrong to base your submission on your bank's position instead of information about the cash market."\xc2\xa0All three cooperators testified that they never contemporaneously told anyone that they thought the\xc2\xa0practice was wrong.

But at trial Connolly and Black said, well, but you haven\xe2\x80\x99t proven that the actual rates they submitted were wrong. Maybe Deutsche Bank could have borrowed at those rates! It\xe2\x80\x99s all subjective guesswork anyway, why not. The trial judge didn't buy it:

The [trial] court stated that \xe2\x80\x9cthe question for the jury was whether Defendants made LIBOR submissions that reflected something other than honestly held estimates of the rate Deutsche Bank would have accepted in order to borrow funds.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\xa6

The court stated that \xe2\x80\x9cwhether Deutsche Bank could have borrowed funds\xc2\xa0at a submitted rate is not dispositive of the falsity of its LIBOR submissions.\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0It ruled that the government was not required to prove "that Deutsche Bank could not have borrowed funds at a rate submitted to the BBA" in order "to establish the falsity of its LIBOR submissions," and that "[a]s a factual matter, even if Deutsche Bank could have borrowed funds at a submitted rate, that would not prevent the submission from being false and misleading."\xc2\xa0The court stated that even "evidence that Deutsche Bank could have borrowed funds at a submitted rate would not have rendered the Defendants'\xc2\xa0statements truthful."

If the BBA had called up Deutsche Bank and said \xe2\x80\x9chey what is the rate at which you\xc2\xa0could borrow funds, were you to do so by asking for and then accepting inter-bank offers in reasonable market size just prior to 11 a.m. London time,\xe2\x80\x9d and Deutsche Bank\xc2\xa0had said \xe2\x80\x9c0.525%,\xe2\x80\x9d and it could in fact have borrowed funds at that rate in the interbank market just prior to 11 a.m. London time , but its submitter incorrectly thought that it would have had to pay 0.545%, then its Libor submission would be \xe2\x80\x9cfalse,\xe2\x80\x9d in the sense that the submitter was trying to be dishonest , even though he gave a factually true answer. In fact it didn\xe2\x80\x99t even matter whether the submitter thought the bank could have borrowed at that rate: What matters is that the submitter\xe2\x80\x99s motive was to make money on derivatives rather than to answer the hypothetical question in the usual unbiased way. Or so said the trial judge.

The Second Circuit disagreed:

The precise hypothetical question to which the LIBOR submitters were responding was\xc2\xa0at what interest rate "could" DB borrow a typical amount of cash if it were to seek interbank offers and were to accept. If the rate submitted is one that the bank could request, be offered, and accept, the submission, irrespective of its motivation, would not be false.

Defendants, of course, had no burden to produce any evidence. The burden was on the government to provide evidence to show that the LIBOR\xc2\xa0submissions made by King or Curtler after receiving requests from Connolly or Black\xc2\xa0were untrue. \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0Yet none of the witnesses testified that DB could not have\xc2\xa0borrowed a typical amount of cash at the rate stated in any of DB's LIBOR\xc2\xa0submissions.

And it noted that actually there were a lot of different rates at which Deutsche Bank could have borrowed:

Most importantly, the one-true-interest-rate theory was also belied by the evidence that loans may have different rates of interest simply because they involve different amounts of principal. King testified that the cash desk would "borrow money every single day,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0and that "[t]here were periods where I need to\xc2\xa0borrow some $20- to $25 billion a day." He said that "[o]ften it costs you\xc2\xa0more to borrow more cash than less cash," and thus loans in various principal\xc2\xa0amounts could be at varying rates of interest.

Similarly, Curtler testified that "there were days where there would have\xc2\xa0been a wide range of offered rates." He said that "[i]f two counterparties were willing to lend to you, I believe I would borrow the cheapest\xc2\xa0money first"; but "[y]ou wouldn't borrow one or the other. You would borrow both . . . ." And the BBA LIBOR Instruction does not say which of those two prices should be submitted.

So in theory on any particular day there could have been a wide range of answers to the question \xe2\x80\x9cwhat is the rate at which you could borrow funds in reasonable size,\xe2\x80\x9d and if the submitter pushed his number up or down by a few basis points to favor a derivatives trader\xe2\x80\x99s positions, he might still end up with a number in that range \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0a number that was\xc2\xa0a factually correct answer to the question.[7]

What a great opinion. Intuitively this makes a lot\xc2\xa0of sense. Libor asked banks to make up a number. The banks made up numbers. Prosecutors decided in hindsight that some of these made-up numbers were \xe2\x80\x9ctrue\xe2\x80\x9d and fulfilled the abstract purpose of Libor, while others were \xe2\x80\x9cfalse\xe2\x80\x9d and constituted criminal fraud for which\xc2\xa0people should go to prison.[8] But all the numbers were made up! They were all guesses, and the distinction between guesses that were good and guesses that were crimes had nothing to do with their correspondence to objective truth; it was just about which guesses came with embarrassing chats and impure motives.

In other ways it is a strange result. Libor is impossible to manipulate , the opinion suggests, or rather impossible to manipulate criminally. Because it was made up, you could make up anything with any sort of nefarious purpose and\xc2\xa0that would be fine. From the Wall Street Journal :

\xe2\x80\x9cIn some ways, these reversals underscore what a screwed-up benchmark Libor was to begin with, when you are not being asked to submit actual offers or bids, but just hypotheticals,\xe2\x80\x9d said Aitan Goelman, a former director of enforcement for the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, a civil regulator that fined many banks for Libor violations. \xe2\x80\x9cIt almost begged to be manipulated.\xe2\x80\x9d

But what is most interesting to me is not that it might be impossible ever to prove that a bank made a false\xc2\xa0Libor submission on any given day, but that the prosecutors didn\xe2\x80\x99t try . You can sort of understand where they were coming from. This looked bad. The banks paid billions of dollars in fines for it. If you stand up in front of a jury of normal people and said \xe2\x80\x9cthese bank traders made a number higher so that they could make more money\xe2\x80\x9d the jury will jump up and shout \xe2\x80\x9cguilty\xe2\x80\x9d before you can finish the sentence. There is a popular\xc2\xa0narrative of corrupt bankers secretly conspiring to make money by nefarious means, and this story fits that narrative so completely that the prosecutors didn\xe2\x80\x99t even think they had to prove that the bankers were lying!

The MoviePass economy

It\xe2\x80\x99s back !

A venture capital-backed battle is raging in New York City in the burgeoning field of instant delivery. At least six startups, including Gorillas Technologies Ltd., Jokr SARL, Getir Perakende Lojistik AS and Buyk Corp., are vying to win the chance to ferry groceries to customers within 10 to 20 minutes of their order placement on an app.

Prices are similar to grocery stores, discounts are plentiful, and many services don\xe2\x80\x99t have a fee or minimum order, allowing consumers to request a single pint of Ben & Jerry\xe2\x80\x99s delivered to their doorstep. Food-delivery app DoorDash Inc., based in San Francisco, also recently entered the fray in New York City.

While these consumer-friendly offerings have brought surging sales, losses are heavy given the high cost of prolific advertising and paying couriers to hand-deliver potato chips, soap and eggs in a short time frame, industry investors and executives said.

Some of the companies are averaging a loss of over $20 per order when factoring in costs like advertising, those people said. \xe2\x80\xa6

Executives and backers of the companies say losses today are investments in a promising prize. Groceries are already an enormous business, and if one or two of the startups grow to dominate the market for quick groceries, the numbers could eventually turn profitable, they say.

\xe2\x80\x9cIn the early minutes of a plane just taking off, it consumes a lot of gas,\xe2\x80\x9d said Nazim Salur, founder of Istanbul-based Getir, which raised money last summer at a $7.5 billion valuation. Once Getir grows large enough, the business will become profitable, he said, something he has seen firsthand with early Getir locations in Turkey.

Isn\xe2\x80\x99t modern finance amazing? The basic model here is that you imagine the world five years in the future, and you imagine that your company has monopolistic dominance of\xc2\xa0the grocery business in that future, and then you think \xe2\x80\x9cthat seems like it would be profitable,\xe2\x80\x9d and you model a stream of profit that is many billions of dollars every year for the rest of time starting in five years, and you calculate the net present value of that stream of income, and it is\xc2\xa0very large, and so you say \xe2\x80\x9cwell then it makes sense to spend a lot of money giving everyone free groceries for a few years to get us to that future of monopolistic dominance,\xe2\x80\x9d and you go to venture capitalists with that pitch, and they say \xe2\x80\x9cwell we do have a ton of money and we love monopolies,\xe2\x80\x9d and they give you the money, and you buy everyone groceries. And then everyone eats the groceries and is like \xe2\x80\x9cthanks, venture capitalists!\xe2\x80\x9d And then in a year or two you flip from giving them free groceries to charging them for groceries, and either it works or it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t.

We have talked about this model a bunch before ; people make fun of it\xc2\xa0but it remains popular and it\xe2\x80\x99s not at all clear that it's wrong. For the venture capitalists the good outcome here is that you succeed in building a monopoly, jack up prices, squeeze your delivery workers\xe2\x80\x99 pay\xc2\xa0and make a lot of money. From a societal perspective it is possible that the good outcome is \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0you know \xe2\x80\xa6 Saudi sovereign wealth funds just give\xc2\xa0everyone in New York free groceries for a while? There is an argument that this business model is a symptom of investors having too much money, and buying groceries for people seems like as good a use of\xc2\xa0that money as anything else.

Goldman vs. sugar babying

Investment banking is a bit of an unusual job in that it recruits a lot of smart ambitious impressive people who have great career options and really don't like investment banking . There is just widespread acceptance that people who did well at certain universities but don't really know what to do with themselves will spend some time as investment-banking analysts, and some of them will love it and make a career of investment banking, and others will use the training and connections from their analyst program to pursue a career in the broader financial or corporate worlds, and some of them will hate it and write essays about how they should have been sex workers instead. Here is Wade Osei Pryor in the Root :

I quickly realized I had no interest in watching stock markets or obsessing over company performance metrics, but I\xe2\x80\x99d never heard of anyone walking away from Goldman. Two summer internships, one college graduation, and six months of full-time work later, and I\xe2\x80\x99ve still never chosen to start my day with the Wall Street Journal . Above all, I struggle with my own complicity in perpetuating the inequalities of global capitalism. The simplest and most compelling reason I have for not wanting to work at Goldman Sachs, however, is that I feel no sense of passion for finance, which is why, as I write this, I considered a markedly different kind of employment offer. It came in the form of a message from a 50-something on Grindr whom I\xe2\x80\x99ve never met: \xe2\x80\x9cHi Wade. Ken here \xe2\x80\x94 great pic. I\xe2\x80\x99m looking for a boyfriend to spoil with a weekly allowance\xe2\x80\xa6Let me know if you\xe2\x80\x99re interested xx.\xe2\x80\x9d

Ken was but the latest in a seemingly endless string of older men to contact me in hopes of exchanging money for romantic attention. While I\xe2\x80\x99ve never engaged in sex work, I don\xe2\x80\x99t imagine it being too huge a departure from working in finance \xe2\x80\x94 both would demand the shelving of my genuine passions in order to exchange large amounts of emotional, mental, and physical labor for capital. ...

I began to wonder why wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t I consider Ken\xe2\x80\x99s offer? From a purely economic point of view, turning him down would be illogical: Goldman demands far more of my time and energy than I imagine Ken would (for context, I worked over 110 hours the week he messaged me), yet in return they offer only fractions of his proposed weekly allowance. Goldman may provide opportunities for career advancement that being a sugar baby likely wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t, but I might be able to mitigate that concern by taking on multiple Kens and saving for the future. If I\xe2\x80\x99m going to exploit myself for capital, then why wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t I do so in a way that maximizes my return and is least in conflict with my values? ...

I know I\xe2\x80\x99m fortunate to have the option of choosing a profession based solely on how passionate I am about the work, and it would be foolish to let money or status or external validation prevent me taking advantage of that. After my brief six-month stint in finance, I now spend my days as an architecture apprentice at a family-run studio in Genoa, working under two architects I really admire.

He probably could have just gone directly into architecture? But he graduated from Harvard \xe2\x80\x9chaving not yet identified my own calling\xe2\x80\x9d so he went to Goldman instead. And, like so many Goldman analysts, he stayed up all night building spreadsheets and wishing he was doing something else.

I have to say that this essay made me sort of bullish on Goldman Sachs Group Inc.? (Disclosure, I also once worked there.) I suspect that the main thing that Goldman wants out of its college recruiting is to be the destination of choice for smart talented ambitious confident well-connected interesting young people who don\xe2\x80\x99t really know what to do with themselves. If you get enough of those people, you will be able to turn a few of them (all you need!) into good bankers and traders, and you will turn a lot more of them into good private-equity and hedge-fund investors or corporate executives who will think fondly of Goldman and be lucrative clients, all of which is good. But also some of them will end up leaving the industry entirely and becoming\xc2\xa0interesting and sort of decorative; some pop star or football player or distinguished architect or renowned\xc2\xa0sugar baby or master criminal or newsletter writer will pop up in the news and you\xe2\x80\x99ll be able to say \xe2\x80\x9coh I knew him when he was at Goldman.\xe2\x80\x9d That might be good for business, it\xe2\x80\x99s certainly good for recruiting (\xe2\x80\x9ctwo years at Goldman will actually help you become a pop star,\xe2\x80\x9d you tell some kid who\xe2\x80\x99s on the fence), but mostly it is good for your self-esteem. \xe2\x80\x9cI don't just work at a job that pays well,\xe2\x80\x9d you can tell yourself; \xe2\x80\x9cI work at a great general-purpose incubator of talent.\xe2\x80\x9d The interestingness of the alumni pool rubs off on the people who stay.

I have in the past suggested that Goldman might be losing that status as a generic elite finishing school, that post-crisis regulations and the fall in prestige\xc2\xa0of banking and the rise of big tech firms has cut into Goldman\xe2\x80\x99s allure. But here\xe2\x80\x99s this guy!\xc2\xa0The system still works!

Leon Cooperman is not impressed by your Bentley

Here is a Washington Post profile of Leon Cooperman puttering around Boca Raton\xc2\xa0being sad that people don't appreciate billionaires; it contains this little\xc2\xa0scene:

Cooperman walked past an old designer home that was being torn down and rebuilt into a new designer home. He continued up the road toward the clay tennis courts, the spa and the terraced clubhouse. A resident drove by in a new Bentley, and Cooperman waved and then watched the $200,000 car drive on. \xe2\x80\x9cYou get a lot of people who show off their wealth,\xe2\x80\x9d he said, \xe2\x80\x9cbut I could buy and sell that guy 100 times.\xe2\x80\x9d

If I were a frugal billionaire and a reporter was coming to town to profile me, I might buy a Bentley, give it to a friend, and pay him to drive by and wave just so I could say that line in\xc2\xa0the profile. That\xe2\x80\x99s just money well spent.

[1] A minor part of the post-scandal Libor cleanup is that Libor is now run by ICE Benchmark Administration Ltd.

[2] Actually there are other poll-based indexes, though usually with less riding on them than Libor. One that we have discussed a lot is the Georgia Dock index of poultry prices, or \xe2\x80\x9cChicken Libor,\xe2\x80\x9d which had its own manipulation scandal .

[3] It is less relevant to our purposes here, but another important\xc2\xa0part of the Libor scandal is that, during the 2008 financial crisis, banks lied about their borrowing costs not (just) to manipulate derivatives but rather to avoid spooking the market. Libor panel submissions were public, and if three-month dollar Libor was 3.00% one day, and the next day the BBA called one big bank and said \xe2\x80\x9cwhat would you pay to borrow dollars for three months,\xe2\x80\x9d and the bank said \xe2\x80\x9cuh, 7%,\xe2\x80\x9d then everyone would know that that bank was in distress and having trouble funding itself, and that would lead to a run on the bank. So at the worst points of the crisis banks understated their borrowing rates, leading Libor to be systematically depressed for a while. This was less of a focus of enforcement actions than the derivatives-manipulation stuff, because (1) it is less obviously evil and greedy and (2) central bankers and regulators seem to have \xe2\x80\xa6 known about and encouraged it ? \xe2\x80\x9cNot sparking panic in the bank funding markets\xe2\x80\x9d is an important goal of banks, but also of bank regulators, and lying a bit about Libor was for a while an acceptable cost for both of them.

[4] Most notably Tom Hayes, who traded interest rate derivatives for UBS Group AG and Citigroup Inc., manipulated Libor,\xc2\xa0was prosecuted in the U.K. and spent about six years in prison .

[5] The case here involved convictions for wire fraud under 18 U.S.C. 1343 , which involves \xe2\x80\x9cany scheme or artifice to defraud, or for obtaining money or property by means of false or fraudulent pretenses, representations, or promises.\xe2\x80\x9d It is not totally clear that those words require actual lying, which the court discusses on pages 30 to 32 of last week\xe2\x80\x99s opinion .

[6] The BBA question is quoted on page 9 of last week\xe2\x80\x99s Second Circuit opinion .

[7]\xc2\xa0In fact the Libor instructions didn\xe2\x80\x99t even tell banks not to consider their derivatives positions: \xe2\x80\x9cAlthough the government states that the BBA's \xe2\x80\x98instructions did not allow a panel bank, when submitting its honest estimate of its borrowing costs, to consider the submission's effect on the profitability of interest rate swaps or other derivatives positions held by the bank's traders,\xe2\x80\x99 in fact the BBA LIBOR Instruction contained no such prohibition.\xc2\xa0In contrast, the BBA did evince a concern about collusion between panel banks. The BBA LIBOR Instruction expressly stated that \xe2\x80\x98Contributor Banks shall input their rate without reference to rates contributed by other Contributor\xc2\xa0Banks.\xe2\x80\x99\xc2\xa0But there was no similar prohibition against banks' making their LIBOR submissions with consideration of the\xc2\xa0bank's own interest-rate-sensitive derivatives.\xe2\x80\x9d I think you could make an argument that, look, some banks will pay Libor in their derivatives, and others will receive Libor, and they will all have incentives to tweak the rates, and those incentives will roughly balance out and the resulting Libor \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0influenced by derivatives positions \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0will reflect something real and market-driven, though that real market-driven thing might not literally be banks\xe2\x80\x99 unsecured funding costs.

[8] Black and Connolly were actually sentenced to probation, fines and home confinement, and the government appealed their sentences to try to send them to prison.
